blocks.speaker: Kanya
blocks.content: No. Even if their lifespan runs out, the citizens of heaven do not die. They are reborn into new life through the Wine of Ilhwa.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Then the numbers don't add up. If everyone can live forever, why does the population remain the same?

blocks.speaker: Kanya
blocks.content: The lifespans of the citizens are under Ra's control. Strict laws calculate the lifespans of the entire population, which is how the fixed number is maintained.
Immortality and population control were inherently contradictory.
In the end, the key lay in the Wine of Ilhwa.
But just like Guardrak, Kanya avoided explaining it. Whether citizen or heretic, everyone seemed to have their own convictions, but when it came to the Wine of Ilhwa, those convictions fell short.
A contradiction.
The Wine of Ilhwa, as Shirone imagined it, was a colossal contradiction.
Passing through the Valley of the Whirling Serpent and the Forest of the Vulgar, they arrived in heaven.
The group tilted their heads back to look up at the sky.
Compared to the scale of the fortress walls, they were no more than dust. It was a level of grandeur beyond human creation.
Kanya introduced this place as the 73rd District and added that there were 820 districts in the First Heaven alone.
Abandoning all attempts to comprehend it, Shirone reached out to touch the wall. He had assumed it was stone, but it was metal.
The technological prowess of heaven far surpassed the common sense of Shirone and his companions.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Is it really okay for us to enter?

blocks.speaker: Kanya
blocks.content: It should be fine. You're Nephilim. But I can't guarantee anything.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Then what about climbing over the wall?
The idea of scaling a wall that reached the sky seemed impossible, but they had no other options.
However, Kanya shook her head at even that suggestion.

blocks.speaker: Kanya
blocks.content: Nothing can fly in heaven's airspace except authorized drones. You'd be shot down. It's never happened before, but I heard about it from my grandfather. And sneaking in is impossible anyway.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Why? There doesn't seem to be anyone guarding the gate.

blocks.speaker: Kanya
blocks.content: They don't restrict entry. It's a matter of the law. All information in heaven is managed by the angels in the Sixth Heaven, Jebul. They can measure everything from mass fluctuations in each district to the composition of gases in the air, so even if you sneak in, it's pointless. Don't worry too much. Nothing dangerous will happen. This isn't purgatory.
As a citizen, Kanya seemed keen on reassuring Shirone, not wanting to plant any negative impressions of heaven in his mind.
Approaching the gate, they held out a gauntlet, and a red beam of light emerged from a lens to scan its surface.
The gate opened with a sound that shook the world, a noise that couldn't be blocked even by covering their ears.
As the entrance to the 73rd District opened, Shirone and his group followed Kanya into heaven.
The gate closed behind them, and the lens flashed.
The updated information of the transformed 73rd District was transmitted to the Sixth Heaven, Jebul.
Shamain's first impression was one of strangeness.
The residential areas of different races were divided along the streets, each with drastically different cultural styles.
The Norrians' buildings were practical, while the Kergoians' were extravagant. And when they spotted the Mechans' buildings, Shirone and Amy froze in their tracks, speechless.
It was the Magic Warehouse, Istas.
Square containers were stacked in layers, freely shifting positions and intersecting with adjacent buildings.
It was hard to imagine the Mechans had come here for a field trip to a magic school. This was evidence that Mecha's technology had somehow flowed into the original world.
Kanya led them past the Mecha district and into a plaza.
The scene in the plaza, where various races mingled like a cultural melting pot, resembled an international market in a neutral country.

blocks.speaker: Amy
blocks.content: Shirone, look over there.
Amy pointed at a statue of a giant.
It felt different from the ones they'd seen at the Kergo altar. Here, giants actually lived. And they had even fought them.
Why would a giant, revered by the citizens, wander purgatory?
Kanya's house was the same size as the other Mecha homes.
If the Kergo sought extravagance and the Norr practicality, the Mecha seemed to prioritize standardization.
Entering Kanya's house, the building rose to four stories.
Despite its inhuman exterior, the interior was cozy.
Her parents, seated at the dining table, sprang to their feet.

blocks.speaker: Kanya's mother
blocks.content: Where on earth have you been? Do you have any idea how worried I was?

blocks.speaker: Kanya
blocks.content: I'm sorry. There were… circumstances.

blocks.speaker: Kanya's mother
blocks.content: And who are these children? They look like Norrians.

blocks.speaker: Kanya
blocks.content: We went to Norr's Shelter. To get this.
When Kanya held out the potion, her mother's eyes shook with shock.

blocks.speaker: Kanya's mother
blocks.content: You… this is…

blocks.speaker: Kanya
blocks.content: It's a stimulant, made with Norr's magic. Drink it before the Wine of Ilhwa.

blocks.speaker: Kanya's mother
blocks.content: Rena! Why would you do this? I never asked for this!

blocks.speaker: Kanya
blocks.content: So you'd just stand by and watch? You went to purgatory to get medicine for Grandpa, didn't you?

blocks.speaker: Kanya's mother
blocks.content: Who told you that…!

blocks.speaker: Kanya
blocks.content: Grandpa did! That's why he could smile until the end, when he entered the Wine of Ilhwa! Isn't that enough? That's what family is, isn't it?

blocks.speaker: Kanya's mother
blocks.content: Why are you like this! Then you must also know that Mom's lifespan was shortened because of that medicine! How do you think I'd feel if my daughter did the same thing!

blocks.speaker: Kanya's father
blocks.content: Both of you, stop. What's done is done. At least they're back safely.
After calming his wife, he turned to Shirone's group.

blocks.speaker: Kanya's father
blocks.content: So, are you heretics? From Norr's Shelter?

blocks.speaker: Kanya
blocks.content: No. They're from the Land of Earth. They saved me and Rena and helped us get this medicine. We might have died without them.

blocks.speaker: Kanya's father
blocks.content: No parent could remain cold to those who had saved their child.

blocks.speaker: Kanya's father
blocks.content: Her father invited Shirone's group inside.
While her mother prepared a meal, Kanya recounted their experiences in purgatory.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: When the topic of Shirone being a Nephilim came up, both parents knelt, causing a commotion in the living room.

blocks.speaker: Kanya's father
blocks.content: Only after the group's earnest pleas did they agree to treat them normally.

blocks.speaker: Kanya's father
blocks.content: I see. Thank you for helping Kanya and Rena. But it'd be best for you to leave soon. Even as Nephilim, those from the Land of Earth won't be welcomed here.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: No. We plan to go to the Seventh Heaven.

blocks.speaker: Kanya's father
blocks.content: Araboat, the sacred land of the gods, was so holy that even speaking of going there could be considered blasphemy. But Shirone, free from the law, could say whatever came to mind.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: But… there's no way to reach the Seventh Heaven.

blocks.speaker: Kanya's father
blocks.content: We'll figure that out. More importantly, I'm curious about the law here. What does it mean for a lifespan to be shortened?

blocks.speaker: Kanya's father
blocks.content: Exactly what it sounds like. Citizens are assigned lifespans at birth according to the law. Kanya's lifespan is 62 years. Rena can live until 73.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Huh? Rena's the younger sister, but her lifespan is longer?

blocks.speaker: Kanya's father
blocks.content: Lifespans can change at any time. Rena gained 10 extra years because she sang hymns beautifully as a child. It's a good thing, but the problem is my wife. Her lifespan is 43 years and 247 days. Only one day remains.
Shirone's heart sank.
Who in the world could know their lifespan down to the day? The mere thought was horrifying.
Yet Kanya's mother was smiling. She was confident in Ra's grace.

blocks.speaker: Kanya's mother
blocks.content: My wife lost 20 years of her lifespan as punishment for venturing into purgatory in her youth. So tomorrow, she'll enter the Wine of Ilhwa.
The idea of altering a person's lifespan was cruel. No, it was hard to even comprehend.
If Ra controlled lifespans, how old was he?
The history of heaven spanned hundreds of thousands of years. Perhaps Ra truly was a god.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: What is the Wine of Ilhwa?

blocks.speaker: Kanya's father
blocks.content: Hmm, they didn't tell you? Well, I suppose they wouldn't. It's not something you need to know. Besides, aren't Nephilim free from the law? It's none of your concern.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: But I still want to know. If I understand it, I might think of a way to help.

blocks.speaker: Kanya's father
blocks.content: Hmm, well then…
All shall heed me! I am Peopene, overseer of the Mecha.
Shirone and his group sprang to their feet.
The voice reached them as if amplified by magic. What was most surprising was that the language was automatically translated.
The ability of telepathy, cast without a channel, using only sound.
If it was magic, it was high-level.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: So it's finally happened. A fairy.

blocks.speaker: Kanya's father
blocks.content: A fairy? Like the ones I know?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Shirone thought of the fairies from his original world.

blocks.speaker: Kanya's father
blocks.content: Yes. The enforcers of the law. If giants uphold the law, fairies are the ones who execute it. You could call them administrators. She's probably here because of Kanya and Rena.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: What should we do?

blocks.speaker: Kanya's father
blocks.content: If you're a Nephilim, you needn't worry. You're free from the law. But… I'm not sure she'll take that into account. She's… unique.
What are you doing? Citizens of this house, open the door at once!
The fairy's voice echoed outside.
They were on the fourth floor. And the fairies Shirone knew couldn't fly.
When her father opened the door, defying Shirone's expectations, a tiny creature flew in.
A girl the size of a palm.
She had golden curly hair, and her delicate features were strikingly clear even on her small face.
She wore green boots that reached her ankles, and her transparent wings fluttered at an invisible speed.

blocks.speaker: Peope
blocks.content: I am the Spiral Fairy, Peope. I have come in person to conduct a citizen trial.
Fairies were born from the essence of lanterns, but they were not of the natural element—they were concepts.
Singular conceptual entities.
Thus, fairies possessed reason and could dominate the concepts from which they originated.

blocks.speaker: Peope
blocks.content: Therefore, I shall now execute the law. Kanya and Rena, have you ventured into purgatory?

blocks.speaker: Kanya
blocks.content: Yes. We went and returned.
Kanya's parents clenched their eyes shut.
But Kanya herself remained calm. She had prepared for this since before leaving heaven.
The only problem was… Rena.

blocks.speaker: Kanya
blocks.content: I'm sorry. With my mother's Wine of Ilhwa approaching, I wanted to serve her delicious food, so I hunted in the Forest of the Vulgar.

blocks.speaker: Peope
blocks.content: Hmph, how dare you lie. What use is food to a citizen who will die tomorrow?
Kanya's mother finally broke into tears.
Even if the Wine of Ilhwa was a blessing, what living being wouldn't fear death? Peope's words had stirred up those fragile emotions.
Shirone felt anger at that.
The citizens called the Wine of Ilhwa a rebirth. But the words of Peope, an administrator, were a clear death sentence.
It wasn't mere intimidation, because there was no malice in Peope's tone.
It was like the honest expression of a naive child's feelings.

blocks.speaker: Peope
blocks.content: Regardless, I shall deliver the verdict. Kanya and Rena, your lifespans shall be reduced by 40 years each.
Kanya's face went blank.
A 40-year reduction meant she had less than 10 years left. Rena was better off, but even so, she would die at 33.
A 40-year reduction for unauthorized departure was the harshest penalty she'd ever heard of.
A year ago, her peer Sael had gotten 5 years. Uncle Kai, a Norrian, got 7. Even her mother, the unluckiest case she knew, only lost 20.
But 40 years? She could accept it for herself, but what about Rena's life?
